title: Bridging Guarded State to a Reactive Property
narrative:
A `Guarded` is designed to be *written by many threads*. A Sprouts property
    (`Var` / `Val` / `Viewable`), on the other hand, is designed to be *owned by a
    single thread*: its change listeners fire synchronously on whatever thread calls
    `set(..)`. Naively sharing a property across threads is therefore unsafe.

    The `Guarded.viewOn(Executor)` method bridges the two worlds. It returns a
    read-only `Viewable` whose value mirrors the guarded container, but with every
    update delivered through an `Executor` you provide. As long as that executor is
    *effectively single-threaded*, the property and all of its listeners run on that
    one owner thread — no matter which background thread mutated the `Guarded`.

    This is the `Guarded` analogue of `observeOn(scheduler)` in Rx-style libraries, or
    a conflated `StateFlow` in Kotlin: a safe, latest-wins, thread-confined live view
    of shared state. It is the idiomatic way to feed background state into a UI thread
    (e.g. the Swing EDT).

    Three rules keep it safe, and this specification both verifies them and serves as a
    usage guide:

    1. The executor must be effectively single-threaded — it defines the owner thread.
    2. The guarded value must be immutable — it crosses threads.
    3. Delivery is conflated (latest-wins) — bursts collapse to the most recent value.

features.id: A view starts out mirroring the current value of the Guarded.

extraInfo:
`viewOn(..)` returns a `Viewable` seeded with the container's current value. The view is a
            read-only `Val`, so you can read it directly — no waiting required for the initial value.

blocks.text: A guarded immutable value and a single-threaded executor.
We create a view of the guarded state.
It immediately reflects the current value.

features.id: A change to the Guarded is propagated to the view.

extraInfo:
When the guarded value changes — from any thread — the view re-synchronises asynchronously
            through its executor. Here we mutate on the test thread and wait for the value to arrive.

blocks.text: A guarded account viewed on a single-threaded executor.
We update the guarded value.
The view eventually reflects the new value.

features.id: The view and its listeners run on the executor thread, not the writer thread.

extraInfo:
This is the whole point of `viewOn(..)`: confinement. A listener registered on the view is
            invoked on the executor's thread, even though the mutation happened on a completely
            different thread. That is what makes it safe to touch single-threaded resources (like a GUI
            toolkit) from inside the listener.

blocks.text: A guarded value, a named single-threaded executor, and a view.
A listener that records which thread it was called on.
We mutate the guarded value from the test thread.
The listener eventually runs — and it ran on the executor thread.

features.id: Delivery is conflated: a burst of updates collapses to the latest value.

extraInfo:
If writes outpace the executor, intermediate values are skipped — the view always converges
            to the *most recent* value rather than queuing every step. We prove this deterministically by
            first blocking the executor's only thread, firing many updates while it is blocked, and then
            releasing it: exactly one delivery happens, carrying the final value.

blocks.text: A guarded counter viewed on a single-threaded executor.
A listener counting how many deliveries actually occur.
We occupy the executor thread with a blocking task before any update is scheduled.
We fire a whole burst of updates while the executor is blocked...
...and only then release the executor.
Exactly one delivery happens, and it carries the final value of the burst.

features.id: The latest value is never lost, even when updates race the delivery.

extraInfo:
Conflation must never *lose* the final value: whatever the interleaving, the view must end up
            equal to the last value written. We hammer the guarded value from several threads and then
            assert the view converges to the final state.

blocks.text: A guarded counter and a view.
Many updates are applied, ending on a known final value.
The view converges to exactly the final value.

features.id: A single Guarded can feed several independent views on different threads.

extraInfo:
Shared state often has more than one consumer. Each `viewOn(..)` call creates an independent,
            self-contained view; a change is fanned out to all of them, each on its own executor thread.

blocks.text: One guarded value observed by two views on two different executors.
We change the guarded value once.
Both views eventually observe the change.

features.id: Dropping a view lets it be garbage collected: the Guarded only references it weakly.

extraInfo:
There is no `unsubscribe` to remember. The container references each view only *weakly*, so
            once you drop your reference, the view (with its listeners) becomes eligible for collection
            and quietly stops receiving updates. This prevents the classic listener leak.

blocks.text: A guarded value and a view, of which we keep only a weak reference.
We drop the strong reference to the view and coax the garbage collector.
code: var collected = Wait.until({ System.gc(); weakView.get() == null }, 5_000)

blocks.text: The view was reclaimed — the Guarded did not keep it alive.
The Guarded remains perfectly usable afterwards.

features.id: After a view is collected, further mutations are harmless and self-prune the dead view.

extraInfo:
Once a view has been collected, the next mutation notices its dead subscription and removes it.
            The mutation itself must never fail because a view disappeared.

blocks.text: A guarded value with a view we immediately drop.
We make sure it is actually collected.
We keep mutating the guarded value.
Nothing throws and the value is correct.

features.id: Creating a view requires a non-null executor.

extraInfo:
A single `viewOn(Executor)` covers every case — the view inherits the container's type and
            null policy — so the only thing it validates eagerly is the executor.

blocks.text: A guarded value and an executor.
We pass a null executor.
It is rejected.

features.id: The view inherits the container type, even when it is a declared supertype.

extraInfo:
Because the container knows its own type (like `Var`), the view simply inherits it. If you
            want the view typed as a supertype, declare the container that way with
            `Guarded.of(SuperType.class, value)`.

blocks.text: A guarded value declared as a supertype.
The view starts in sync and reports the inherited type.
We mutate the guarded value.
The view tracks it.

features.id: A nullable container yields a null-permitting view, viewable even while it currently holds null.

extraInfo:
With the redesign there is no separate nullable view method: the nullability flows from the
            container. A `Guarded.ofNull(..)` / `Guarded.ofNullable(..)` container produces a view that
            permits null, and — unlike before — it can be viewed even while it currently holds null,
            faithfully publishing both null and non-null values as they come and go.

blocks.text: A nullable guarded value that starts out null.
The view starts empty (null) and reports itself nullable.
We publish a non-null value.
The view reflects it.
We then publish null again.
The view goes back to null.

features.id: A fallback view of a nullable container is null-safe: it shows the fallback instead of null.

extraInfo:
`viewOn(Executor, fallback)` turns a nullable container into a null-safe view — the
            `Guarded` analogue of `Val.view(nullObject, ..)`. Whenever the container holds null, the view
            publishes the fallback instead, so the view is non-null and never empty.

blocks.text: A nullable guarded that starts out null, viewed with a fallback.
The view starts on the fallback (not null) and is non-null.
We publish a real value.
The view shows it.
We go back to null.
The view falls back again rather than going null.

features.id: A fallback view validates its fallback eagerly.

extraInfo:
The fallback is published, so it must be non-null and assignable to the container's type;
            both are checked eagerly.

blocks.text: We pass a null fallback.
It is rejected.
We pass a fallback that is not assignable to the type (bypassing generics, as raw code can).
It is rejected.
